tweets.full_text:
おはようございます。

Claude Codeを触り始めて一番変わったのは、「朝イチ、何からやろう」が無くなったこと。
昨日のうちに『これやっといて』と頼んでおけば、起きた時にはもう進んでる。

非エンジニアでも、ここまで来れる時代になった。

tweets.full_text: Claude Codeのauto modeの話、実務的ですね。AIエージェントが強力になるほど安全設計が問われるという指摘は本当そこです。自動化の利便性と安全性のバランスは、n8nとかのワークフロー自動化でも同じ課題ですが、hooksで自動検査する層を持つ設計はスケールする時に必須になってくる気がします。

user.name: まぁる＠YouTube×AI自動化の設計士
user.description: 【AIが勝手に伸びる型を発見】YouTube自動化設計士 まぁる  感情論ゼロのデータ分析｜登録者超え動画だけ自動収集・解析 文字起こし貼るだけでAIが「視聴者心を掴む構成」自動生成  「伸びる動画の型」をnote期間限定公開中🔥 ↓今すぐ全公開チェック

quoted_status.full_text:
Claude Codeのauto modeは「全部おまかせ」ではなく、許可判断をどう安全に自動化するかの話。

実務ではここが大事です。
・守ってほしい前提: CLAUDE.md
・何度も使う手順: skills
・自動で止める/検査する: hooks

AIエージェントは、強くするほど安全装置の設計が重要になります。 https://t.co/o0wTXmqYgf

tweets.full_text:
Claude Fable 5 is goated with design and a lot more other things

The only thing is, I don't like to spend limits on /fast mode, and the slow/normal mode in Claude code is A LOT slower than it is for GPT for e.g

With GPT 5.5 I feel like the normal mode does stuff a lot faster.

It might be that the model itself Fable 5 is slower than usual, as this wasn't the case with other Opus 4.6 for e.g

tweets.full_text:
Last week at NY Tech Week, I did a live demo around a problem almost every financial CTO I talk to is dealing with right now.

Analysts are building apps faster than anyone can review them. Vibe-coded dashboards everywhere. No access controls, no audit trail, no way to share work across teams easily.

The event ran under the Chatham House Rule, so we didn’t record it. But enough people asked afterwards that I re-recorded the full demo.

A few things you’ll see in it:
- An admin view where changing a user’s role instantly changes which apps and widgets they can access.
- A risk dashboard shared across users, duplicated, remixed, and shared back.
- A vendor app marketplace where I subscribe to a Kalshi App in 3 clicks.
- Claude Code driving the OpenBB Workspace MCP to analyze that Kalshi app (and the very same app was built with the Workspace MCP in the first place!)

The theme tying it all together: everything is converging into one centralized interface where work happens.

user.name: Elliott Crosby
user.description: Founder of Nodea, the branching AI chat canvas. Mechanical engineering @ Virginia Tech. I build and ship solo.

tweets.full_text:
if you're still typing every prompt by hand in Claude Code, you are the loop

and loops are supposed to run without you

loop engineering is where you stop being the operator and start being the architect

you define what "done" looks like, hand it to claude, and walk away

claude runs itself. checks its own output, spawns subagents for parallel work, retries failed steps - all without a single message from you

you come back to finished work, not a stalled output waiting on your next input

four things that make a loop actually run:

> a task file with a real completion condition (not "do X", but "done when Y is true")
> a self-verification step claude can run without asking you
> subagent triggers so parallel tracks don't queue up sequentially
> a hard stop clause so it doesn't spin on a broken assumption

without those four, you're not doing loop engineering. you're using a very fast autocomplete and doing all the real work yourself

devs who figured this out two months ago are shipping entire features overnight

they're not smarter. they're not working more hours. they just stopped being the loop

most people are still babysitting. the ones who aren't are already lapping them

tweets.full_text:
Last week I got early access to Fable 5, Anthropic's new model. I've been sitting with one thing it did, because it taught me the single most important lesson about how to work with this model.

Fable 5 built its own PowerPoint for me. Not a deck — the PowerPoint app. 

I pointed it at a problem, and instead of just solving it, it built the tool that solves it: a CLI version of PowerPoint. I open sourced it today. It's called Hands-on Deck.

But the tool isn't really the point. How it got built is.

Here's the short version. I'm on the consulting team at Every, and we make a LOT of decks. We'd been using Anthropic's PPTX skill for months — great on a good day, but on a bad day the agent gets buried doing "markup archaeology" across thousands of lines of XML just to nudge one text box. When Fable 5 dropped, I tested it on a simple 6-slide deck. It burned 30 million tokens and 20 minutes — and barely looked better.

So instead of asking it to make a better deck, I asked it to step back and improve the skill it was running inside.

That's the whole lesson.

This model shines when you give it the task behind the task. Don't point it at the immediate work in front of you — take a step back, think of the more ambitious version of what you actually want, and point Fable at that. Ask it to fix the system, not the output. That's when it stops being a faster you and starts being something else entirely.

When I did that, Fable diagnosed the real problem — "the cost isn't the editing, it's the XML entering the context window" — and then designed and built the fix itself. 

That's not it. Once it had a tool that didn't drown it in tokens, something I didn't expect happened: it spent the savings on ambition. It started reviewing its own work over and over, fixing tiny details, cross-verifying, unprompted. With the old skill I had to beg it to review once. Now it behaves like a designer instead of a coder.

The full story — the diagnosis, the emergent behavior, the blind evals where this won a clear majority — is in the video. The deck you're watching in it was made entirely by Fable using the tool it built. No template.

Hands-on Deck is open source. Works in Claude Code, Codex, https://t.co/etbkOZLKuX — anywhere you can add a skill or a CLI.

But the real takeaway isn't the tool. It's this: next time, don't give Fable the task. Give it the task behind the task. This model is limited by your ambition and creativity.
 
📷 https://t.co/glwhGE9et7

And join the power user camp below for more tricks from the @every team!

tweets.full_text:
There are 60+ slash commands built into Claude Code. Most people stop at /clear and /help.

A few you probably haven't met: /rewind undoes code and conversation back to a checkpoint. /context shows exactly what's eating your context window. /goal registers a finish line and Claude keeps working toward it across turns.

And the real unlock is the type you write yourself. A custom command is just a Markdown file: a few lines of config on top, plain English instructions below. One catch: it isn't technically a command anymore. It's a skill, and that difference is where the leverage lives.

This is chapter 1 of my 10-part Claude Code course. The whole chapter is in this thread, slide by slide. 🧵

tweets.full_text:
I built markupmarkdown with Claude Code, for teams workaround with .md files:

Markupmarkdown is Google-Docs-style review on top of any markdown file, straight from GitHub. Drag-select to comment, threaded replies, @-mentions, realtime sync. Click Edit for an editor with formatting toolbar + find/replace + live preview. Click Push to GitHub for a PR or direct commit. Video shows the whole loop.

Why? A lot of real product thinking lives in markdown now. CLAUDE.md for your repo. SKILL.md for your agents. PRDs Claude wrote you. RFCs, prompt libraries, release notes.  That's a lot of .md, and the moment any of it leaves your local checkout the review story collapses:  

 * GitHub PRs are too heavy for "this paragraph isn't quite right"
 * Google Docs strips formatting and forks the source of truth
 * Slack threads die after a day

I added some Claude-specific features too:

There's an MCP server at /mcp. Your Claude Code session reads what you read, leaves threads, replies, edits, opens PRs back. Anything destructive needs explicit human sign-off. Personal access tokens scope agents to read / write / admin. Agent comments carry a visible bot badge so threads stay legible.

In practice: Claude can drop a thread on the very CLAUDE.md you're collaborating on, propose a wording change, and you either resolve-and-revise or reply with feedback. Same surface, both audiences. The spec stays alive instead of going stale after the first commit.

There's also "Revise with AI" — Claude Opus takes the comments you've resolved and produces a clean revision. Word-level diff before you accept. BYO Anthropic key, encrypted at rest.

Bonus: paste github com/<org> into the URL bar and get a filterable, shareable index of every .md across every repo they own. Big orgs spider in <10s. Save filter tabs (claude.md, _PRD, …). Private repos respect your GitHub access on every view.

MIT licensed. Free. Try it on my website or run it self-hosted.

Agentically engineered in Claude Code over a couple of days. Thanks @AnthropicAI for the tools!

Try it live: mumd dot metavert dot io

tweets.full_text:
Tired of hand-placing coordinates for your architecture diagrams? Good news 📐 An agent skill that generates https://t.co/u0fycWTFnb diagrams straight from natural language.

Title: Agents365-ai/drawio-skill
URL: https://t.co/uxW1tANo5G

📐 Overview
drawio-skill turns natural-language descriptions into https://t.co/u0fycWTFnb diagrams (.drawio XML) and exports them to PNG/SVG/PDF/JPG. It can also parse an existing codebase (Python, JavaScript/TypeScript, Go, Rust) and turn it into an auto-laid-out structure diagram.

❓ Challenges Solved
Drawing clean diagrams in https://t.co/u0fycWTFnb has always been quietly painful.
・Hand-placing coordinates: you position each node manually, and large graphs break down
・Guessing vendor icon names: without exact AWS/Azure/GCP shape names you end up with blank boxes
Time went to layout and icon-hunting instead of the actual content — what connects to what.

💡 Methodology & Proposed Approach
drawio-skill offloads the tedious parts of diagramming to the skill itself.
・6 diagram presets: ERD, UML class, sequence, architecture, ML, flowchart
・Resolves 10,000+ official shapes and 321 AI/LLM logos precisely
・Self-checking: it reads its own PNG output and auto-fixes overlaps, clipped labels, and stacked edges
・Up to 5 rounds of iterative refinement
・Auto-layouts code import graphs and class hierarchies deterministically via Graphviz (orthogonal edge routing)

🌍 Use Cases
It's easy to adopt and drops into existing agent setups.
・Works with Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, OpenClaw, and other Agent Skills-format systems
・Ships as a single SKILL.md file and needs only the https://t.co/u0fycWTFnb desktop CLI (no MCP server or background daemon)
・Install the CLI, then add the skill via "npx skills add Agents365-ai/365-skills -g". License is MIT
It substantially speeds up design docs and onboarding materials.

#drawio #AgentSkills

tweets.full_text:
「アーキテクチャ図、もう座標を手で置くのは疲れた」という人に朗報です📐 自然言語からhttps://t.co/u0fycWTFnb図を生成してくれるエージェントスキルが登場しました。

タイトル: Agents365-ai/drawio-skill
URL: https://t.co/uxW1tANo5G

📐 概要
drawio-skillは、自然言語の説明からhttps://t.co/u0fycWTFnb図（.drawio XML）を生成し、PNG/SVG/PDF/JPGへ書き出すスキルです。さらに、既存のコードベース（Python、JavaScript/TypeScript、Go、Rust）を解析して、自動レイアウトの構造図に変換することもできます。

❓ 解決する課題
https://t.co/u0fycWTFnbできれいな図を描くのは、地味に骨の折れる作業でした。
・座標の手配置：ノードを一つひとつ手で置く必要があり、大きなグラフほど破綻しやすい
・ベンダーアイコン名の推測：AWS/Azure/GCPなどの正確なシェイプ名がわからず、結局「空の箱」になってしまう
本質（何と何がどうつながるか）ではなく、レイアウトとアイコン探しに時間を取られていました。

💡 方法論と提案手法
drawio-skillは、図づくりの面倒な部分をスキル側に肩代わりさせます。
・6種類の図プリセット：ERD、UMLクラス図、シーケンス図、アーキテクチャ図、ML図、フローチャート
・10,000以上の公式シェイプと321種類のAI/LLMロゴを正確に解決
・自己チェック機能：自分で出力したPNGを読み返し、重なり・見切れ・線の重複を自動修正
・最大5ラウンドの反復フィードバックで仕上げる
・コードのimportグラフやクラス階層を、Graphvizで決定論的に自動配置（直交エッジルーティング）

🌍 ユースケース
気軽に導入でき、既存のエージェント環境に組み込めます。
・Claude Code、Cursor、Copilot、OpenClawなどAgent Skillsフォーマット対応システムで動作します
・配布は単一のSKILL.mdファイルで、必要なのはhttps://t.co/u0fycWTFnbデスクトップCLIのみ（MCPサーバーや常駐デーモンは不要）
・導入はCLIを入れて「npx skills add Agents365-ai/365-skills -g」など。ライセンスはMITです
設計ドキュメントやオンボーディング資料の作成を大きく効率化します。

#drawio #AIエージェントSkill
